protocol: medical - respiratory distress/asthma/copd/croup/reactive airway (respiratory distress - croup/epiglottits) (protocol 9 - 11)

dialogue.utterance: EMS Unit 3, responding for an infant with breathing issues at the provided address.
Hi sir, I'm Jordan, an EMT with the rescue squad. What led you to call 911 for your son?
He's wheezing a lot and struggling to breathe. This morning, he had a cough and shortness of breath.
Can you tell me your name and that you're with us, buddy?
Mmm... Dada.
That's right. You're in Richmond. What's your name, sweetheart?
Great. Your eyes are open and you're responding well. Let's check your breathing more closely now.
We see you're using belly breathing and breathing rapidly. Do you have a clear airway right now?
He wheezes when breathing out but can speak and cry.
He has a high respiratory rate with wheezing. Would it be okay if we use a nebulizer mask to help his breathing?
His breaths are at 44, which is high for his age.
Oxygen saturation is 93% on room air.
His pulse is 169, very fast, but his peripheral pulses are strong.
Blood pressure isn't being registered-0/0-at this time, which can occur due to respiratory distress.
We're unable to measure glucose at this moment.
Does he seem to be in any pain or discomfort at the moment?
No, he doesn't look in pain at all. He's fussing a little but seems to be managing.
We'll give him Albuterol through a nebulizer to help with his breathing. It's a common treatment for this.
We'll also add Atrovent to increase the effectiveness. You might see a reduction in the wheezing soon.
Let's check his breathing again. His respirations are now at 34.
And oxygen saturation is now up to 100%.
Is his behavior normal otherwise? Does he wheeze often?
No, he was fine until he had his flu shot four days ago. He's not been the same since.
This morning he woke with a cough and trouble breathing. The at-home nebulizer helped for a bit, but it came back when I got home.
What medications has he taken at home?
Only the Albuterol through his nebulizer.
Does he have any known allergies?
No known drug allergies. But his nurse said he wheezes from time to time.
Any other history or conditions you think important?
His chart lists other respiratory disorders.
No signs of bleeding or visible injury to the airway area.
Can I listen to his breath sounds with the mask on?
Go ahead.
There's wheezing on expiration in all lung fields, which indicates airway narrowing.
He currently has a patent airway with no obstruction in sight.
On the Glasgow Coma Scale he is at a 15-awake, alert, and responding for his age.
Is he still comfortable and not in pain?
Yes, he still seems fine.
We'll do a second dose of Albuterol and Atrovent through the nebulizer to try to further open his airways.
Let's check again in a few minutes. His respirations are still around 34.
SpO2 is still 100%, which is great.
His heart rate is down to 157-still fast but improving.
Blood pressure is still unmeasurable, but we'll keep monitoring close. We know it's largely affected in respiratory conditions like this.
We'll now repeat the Albuterol treatment to see if his condition improves further.
Would you prefer us to bring him to VCU or another facility?
VCU is fine. Please take him there as soon as possible.
We'll secure him into the infant car seat using the straps provided in the truck.
Would you be okay if we place him on a 4-lead ECG monitor during transport?
His pulse is still around 157 and steady.
All leads are in place and the monitor is functioning.
All straps are checked. We're ready for transport. Time now is 19:00.
VCU, this is Unit 3 with an infant who's displaying improved respiratory distress. Transport en route in cold condition.
Vital signs now: pulse 157, resp 34, SpO2 100%. Still no complaints, clear and stable.
No changes in his comfort level seen so far.
Let's check breath sounds on expiration again.
Examiner hears continued expiratory wheezing but with less intensity.
We have no indication of pain throughout the care process, and he's not showing signs of pain based on our continuous assessment.
Same observations from me. He just feels a bit worked up.
I'll do a head-to-toe check now. Pupils are equal and reactive.
No neck stiffness or visible trauma. All appears normal for now.
Checking motor response and peripheral pulses in the lower and upper extremities.
Peripheral pulses are strong and symmetric. No visible deformities or lack of use in extremities.
Rechecking SpO2 once more at 100%-maintained well since the last measurement.
To properly document, may I ask for your son's name and age?
His name is James and he is 10 months old.
We've given Albuterol and Atrovent twice so far. No adverse reactions.
Any other medications besides Albuterol given or administered today?
No, only what we've done here.
Since we have improved vitals and no complications, we'll continue the same care for now.
We'll begin transport at 19:00. No EMS complications to report.
Just confirming with radio to VCU that we have a stable pediatric with asthma exacerbation en route.
Just received confirmation from the receiving facility for patient details.
O2 saturation stays at 100% during drive.
His respirations still maintain around 34, less high than before.
Pulse stable at 157, no significant drop noted.
Secured on stretcher and in his car seat properly with shoulder harnesses and side restraints.
VCU, we are approaching your facility.
Vehicle will arrive at receiving entrance in 2 minutes. Patient remains stable.
We are currently 300 feet from your ED. Prep your intake team for a 10-month-old male with improved asthma-like symptoms and breathing.
You'll also need one dose of Albuterol still in the nebulizer.
Thank you for the cooperation from the father and the family.
Patient transferred in stable condition. All provided documentation ready for your intake.
Consistent with ePCR report, he does not exhibit any signs of pain, and patient pain is reported as 0.
Just wanted to note again that repetitive blood pressure efforts fail to record readings due to underlying respiratory distress-0/0 as per ePCR.
Ensured proper straps are intact and frequent checks during transport confirmed stability.
Pre-brief for meds: Albuterol given twice, Atrovent given twice, and oxygen administered via nebulizer.
Per ePCR, there are no documented complications during transport.
Final update-SpO2 100%, respiratory rate 34, heart rate 157, and no breathing or circulatory alarm signs at 19:10.
